whatItMeasures: Total bilirubin measures the combined level of direct (conjugated) and indirect (unconjugated) bilirubin in the blood; bilirubin is a yellow pigment produced when red blood cells break down, and elevated levels cause jaundice.

referenceRanges.population: Newborns (jaundice threshold varies by age in hours — specialist assessment required)
referenceRanges.source: AAP. Management of Hyperbilirubinemia in the Newborn Infant. 2004.

causesOfHigh: Liver disease (hepatitis, cirrhosis, alcoholic liver disease)
Bile duct obstruction (gallstones, cholangitis, pancreatic cancer)
Hemolytic anemia (rapid red cell destruction releases excess bilirubin)
Gilbert's syndrome (benign genetic variant causing mildly elevated indirect bilirubin)
causesOfLow: Low bilirubin is not clinically significant

high: Jaundice — yellowing of the skin and whites of the eyes
Abdominal pain (particularly in bile duct obstruction)
